title: STANDARD FOR WHOLE AND DECORTICATED PEARL MILLET GRAINS

sections.section: STANDARD FOR WHOLE AND DECORTICATED PEARL MILLET GRAINS
sections.summary: Document title only: 'Standard for Whole and Decorticated Pearl Millet Grains'—no substantive section content provided here.
Document reference only: 'CXS 169-1989'—no substantive section content provided here.

sections.section: Adopted in 1989. Revised in 1995. Amended in 2019, 2023.
sections.summary: Administrative adoption/amendment history: standard adopted 1989, revised 1995, and amended in 2019 and 2023.
Amendment notice stating that, following decisions at the Codex Alimentarius Commission Forty-sixth Session (Dec 2023), amendments were made to the Annex.
Scope: standard applies to whole and decorticated pearl millet (Pennisetum americanum L.), including Senegalese varieties 'souna' and 'sanio', intended for human consumption.

Defines pearl millet grains as whole or decorticated and suitably dried with the characteristics of Pennisetum americanum L.; whole grains are those obtained by threshing without mechanical treatment and decorticated grains have outer parts removed (20–22% of whole-grain weight) by mechanical means.

Product definition: pearl millet grains must be whole or decorticated and suitably dried as needed, matching species characteristics of Pennisetum americanum L., with whole grains unmechanically threshed and decorticated grains having ~20–22% outer parts removed by mechanical treatment.
Definition of whole grains: pearl millet grains obtained after proper threshing with no mechanical treatment.
Definition of decorticated grains: pearl millet grains from which outer parts equal to 20–22% of the whole-grain weight have been removed by appropriate mechanical treatment (e.g., abrasion).

Sets essential composition and quality requirements: grains must be safe and suitable for human consumption, free from abnormal flavours/odours, living insects and hazardous filth; specifies a maximum moisture of 13% m/m (with lower limits for certain destinations), defines extraneous matter, and gives tolerances including extraneous-matter limits of 2% for whole and 0.5% for decorticated grains plus practical freedom from dirt, animal debris, mineral particles and diseased grains.

General quality factors: pearl millet grains must be safe and suitable for human consumption and free from abnormal flavours, odours, living insects and filth in amounts that could pose a health hazard.

sections.section: 3.1.1 Pearl millet grains shall be safe and suitable for human consumption.
sections.summary: Requirement: pearl millet grains shall be safe and suitable for human consumption.

sections.section: 3.1.2 Pearl millet grains shall be free from abnormal flavours, odours, and living insects.
sections.summary: Requirement: pearl millet grains shall be free from abnormal flavours, odours, and living insects.

sections.section: 3.1.3 Pearl  millet  grains  shall  be  free  from  filth  (impurities  of  animal  origin,  including  dead  insects)  in  amounts which may represent a hazard to human health.
sections.summary: Requirement: pearl millet grains shall be free from filth (animal-origin impurities, including dead insects) in amounts that may represent a hazard to human health.

Specific quality factor: sets a maximum moisture content of 13% m/m for pearl millet and notes that lower moisture limits may be required for certain destinations depending on climate, transport and storage duration.
Specifies moisture limit: maximum 13% m/m, with recommendation that lower moisture limits be applied for particular destinations according to climate and transport/storage duration.
Defines 'extraneous matter' as vegetable matter, shrivelled (under‑mature) grains, altered grains and similar materials.

Defect tolerances: extraneous matter must not exceed 2% in whole pearl millet and 0.5% in decorticated millet, and both types must be practically free from dirt, animal debris, mineral particles and diseased grains.

Contaminant requirements: pearl millet must be free from hazardous levels of heavy metals and must comply with Codex maximum limits for pesticide residues and mycotoxins established for this commodity.
Heavy metals requirement: pearl millet grains shall be free from heavy metals in amounts that may represent a hazard to human health.
Pesticide residue requirement: pearl millet grains must comply with Codex Alimentarius Commission maximum residue limits established for this commodity.
Mycotoxin requirement: pearl millet grains must comply with Codex maximum mycotoxin limits established for this commodity.

Hygiene requirements: recommends preparing and handling the product in accordance with the Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene and relevant CAC codes; under good manufacturing practice the product should be free from objectionable matter; when tested by appropriate sampling it must not contain microorganisms, parasites, or microbial-origin substances in amounts that may present a hazard to health.

sections.section: 5.1 It is recommended that the product covered by the provisions of this standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the appropriate sections of the General principles of food hygiene (CXC 1-1969), 1  and other codes of practice recommended by the CAC which are relevant to this product.
sections.summary: Recommended that the product covered by this standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969) and other relevant Codex codes of practice.

sections.section: 5.2 To the extent possible in good manufacturing practice, the product shall be free from objectionable matter.
sections.summary: Obligation that, to the extent practicable under good manufacturing practice, the product shall be free from objectionable matter.

sections.section: 5.3 When tested by appropriate methods of sampling and examination, the product:
sections.summary: When tested by appropriate sampling and examination, the product must be free from microorganisms and parasites and must not contain substances originating from microorganisms in amounts that may represent a hazard to health.

Packaging requirements for pearl millet grains: containers must protect hygienic, nutritional, technological and organoleptic qualities; packaging materials must be safe and not impart toxic substances or undesirable odours/flavours; sacks, if used, must be clean, sturdy and securely sewn or sealed.

Labelling provisions supplementing the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods (CXS 1-1985): specifies product naming requirements and that for non-retail containers the product name, lot identification and manufacturer/packer name and address must appear on the container or in accompanying documents, with lot identification and name/address allowed to be replaced by an identifiable mark provided it links clearly to the accompanying documents.

sections.section: 7.1.1 The name of the product to be shown on the label shall be 'millet grains', or 'decorticated millet grains'.
sections.summary: Mandatory product name on the label: either 'millet grains' or 'decorticated millet grains'.

(Duplicate labelling section) In addition to CXS 1-1985, specifies that the product name and, for non-retail containers, the product name, lot identification and manufacturer/packer name and address must appear on the container or be provided in accompanying documents, with limited allowance for an identifiable mark.

Methods of analysis and sampling: compliance shall be checked using the methods in CXS 234-1999 relevant to this standard and users should specify the appropriate limit and method where multiple options exist; the section also lists quality factor limits for millet (appearance by visual exam, 1000-kernel weight ranges, 1 litre weight range, ash 0.8–1.0% d.b., protein minimum 8.0% d.b., decortication max 20%, crude fibre and fat ranges for whole and decorticated grains).
